coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional network space Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH for enhanced organic pollutant photodegradation: Synergetic photocatalysis activity/stability and effect of real water quality parameters
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhuo
Li, Xiaoming
Xu, Qiuxiang
Tao, Ziletao
Yao, Fubing
Huang, Xiaoding
Wu, You
Wang, Dongbo
Jiang, Pinghong
Yang, Qi
coredata.dc:description:
In this report, a N, P co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) modified silver phosphate united graphene hydrogel (Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH) photocatalyst with three-dimensional network space structure was designed to build a synergetic photocatalytic activity/stability effect for organic pollutants degradation, which could be confirmed by characterization and photodegradation performance experiments. Convert-light via N, P-CQDs exactly matched for Ag3PO4 could induce more photocarrier generation, meanwhile the N, P-CQDs as an electron acceptor could accelerate the charge separation for anti-photocorrosion. This special network space structure in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst provided more reaction active sites to accelerate mineralized reaction process. The photodegradation efficiency of TC in composed wastewater and real wastewater were tested with fresh and used Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH as photocatalyst. Specifically, the tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal rate with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (6%) was found to increase by 20% that of Ag3PO4 and 14% that of N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4, and the organic pollutants removal efficiency with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst maintained at 91.5% at 6th cyclic experiment. Meanwhile, the fresh and after photodegradation efficiency of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (6%) was maintained over 75% in real water matrix. The possible degradation pathway of TC was explored and the photodegradation mechanism was verified by the predominant induce active species (
O2 − and h+) in the photocatalytic system. As the consequence, the 3D network space structure Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH could be applied for sustaining organic pollutants photodegradation in the natural environment production.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this report, a N, P co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, P-CQDs) modified silver phosphate united graphene hydrogel (Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH) photocatalyst with three-dimensional network space structure was designed to build a synergetic photocatalytic activity/stability effect for organic pollutants degradation, which could be confirmed by characterization and photodegradation performance experiments. Convert-light via N, P-CQDs exactly matched for Ag3PO4 could induce more photocarrier generation, meanwhile the N, P-CQDs as an electron acceptor could accelerate the charge separation for anti-photocorrosion. This special network space structure in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst provided more reaction active sites to accelerate mineralized reaction process. The photodegradation efficiency of TC in composed wastewater and real wastewater were tested with fresh and used Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH as photocatalyst. Specifically, the tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal rate with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (6%) was found to increase by 20% that of Ag3PO4 and 14% that of N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4, and the organic pollutants removal efficiency with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst maintained at 91.5% at 6th cyclic experiment. Meanwhile, the fresh and after photodegradation efficiency of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (6%) was maintained over 75% in real water matrix. The possible degradation pathway of TC was explored and the photodegradation mechanism was verified by the predominant induce active species (
O2 − and h+) in the photocatalytic system. As the consequence, the 3D network space structure Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH could be applied for sustaining organic pollutants photodegradation in the natural environment production.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the environment-friendly sustainable technology, photocatalysis with semiconductor (Narrow band gap) such as Ag3PO4 photocatalyst has been successfully applied for organic pollutants decomposition [1,2]. However, in many cases, its application was limited by the photocatalysis activity and stability of photocatalysts, which are generally in a trade-off relation. When Ag3PO4 was used as photocatalyst, the performance was restricted for its narrow absorbance wavelength region and easy photo-corrosion, during which the silver can be reduced by the photo-generated electrons (4Ag3PO4 + 6H2O + 12 h++12e− → 12Ag + 4H3PO4 + 3O2). Ag3PO4-based photocatalysts with varies pattern including Ag3PO4/Ti3C2 [3], SPR effect in Ag3PO4 [4], modified Ag3PO4-based multiple structure [5,6], etc. have been studied extensively, while few research have been done on broadening wavelength region, which normally made semiconductor excite more photocarrier possible, and obtained a higher carrier recombination rate of Ag3PO4 [7]. The full-spectrum response and anti-photocorrosion were seldom been taking into consideration. Therefore, it is critical to find an effective photocatalyst with dual function of high photocatalysis activity and stability.

The micrometer-scale graphene hydrogel (rGH) with high specific surface area and low porosity allows molecules to diffuse freely through the layer [8]. Owing to the π-π conjugation of laminar graphene nano-sheets, the unique hierarchical architecture in the rGH can be assembled [9]. Besides, mechanically strong and stability network materials simplified the filter system and made the separation free [10,11]. It has been identified that the stable rGH could eliminate the drawback of the graphene for its low conjunction interface and easy being oxidized, meanwhile maintain the easy π-π stacking interaction from graphene [12,13]. Very recently, the polymer gels have been exploited as varied reaction vessels to develop photocatalytic reusability [14]. Ag3PO4/rGH compose has been successfully employed in continuous flow reaction test to prove its stability [15]. However, the shadowing effect of rGH could decrease the photocatalysis activity. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could broaden the response wavelength of semiconductors more easily due to its excellent photoluminescence (PL) including down conversion photoluminescence (DCPL) and up conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) [16]. Notably, Su et al. prepared N and P co-doped CQDs with excitation/emission maxima at 360/430 nm [17], which the conversion wavelength could help the Ag3PO4 extend the utilization of the light source. It has been reported that the carbon quantum dots acted as the photo-induced electron conveyor and electron-storage trap at the same time [18]. Furthermore, the zero-dimensional carbon nanostructure could be assembled freely due to unique π-conjugation and full of oxygen-rich functional groups [19]. The combination of N, P-CQDs with Ag3PO4 and rGH might construct an advanced photocatalyst system with synergetic photocatalytic activity and stability.

Herein, a reasonable strategy of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH with 3D network space structures photocatalyst was successfully designed in this report. The photocatalytic activity and the recyclable capability were investigated. A series interferential condition of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) removal was made an examination on photocatalysts dosage, initial TC concentrations, content of rGH, light irradiation. The effect of important water quality parameters was analyzed to confirm the degradation performance of photocatalysts in practical application. The photodegradation mechanism was studied simultaneously. The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH possess easy recovery utilization, outstanding photostability as well as strong synergistic photocatalysis and absorption performance for organic contaminant degradation.

All chemicals (analytic grade reagents) were purchased from Ainopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd The large-scale synthesis of N, P-CQDs were prepared by simple electrochemical oxidation treatment with minor modifications [17]. AMP (0.5 g) was dispersed well in 50 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring. Then, the transparent solution used as electrolyte was transferred to an electrochemical oxidation system (graphite rods as anode and cathode). After the clear yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtered by a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove the precipitated graphene particles, the N, P-CQDs was dried through vacuum freeze-dryer. The N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 as binary catalysts was synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal process. The N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 as binary catalysts was synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal process. Firstly, Na3PO4·12H2O (1 mol) and AgNO3 (3.0 mol) was simultaneously dissolved in 8 mL deionized water by a coprecipitation method. The suspension was mixed with polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000) and N, P-CQDs powders under vigorous stirring for 30 min when mixture turned into green-yellow sediment. The mixture solution reacted under the hydrothermal conditions at 393.15 K for 12 h. The resultant was removed residual impurity and the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 dehydrated at 60 ℃ until desiccation. All prepared powder materials stored at drying cabinet at 25℃, 30% RH before further experiments.

Graphene oxide (GO) with low defects was synthesized via the modification of Hummer’s methods [20]. A certain amount of GO was dispersed in 20 mL deionized water through ultrasonication over 1 h. The mixtures of GO solution and solid N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 with two drops of PEG-200 were grinded for 5 min until the slurry was homogenous. After stirring in water-bath (50 ℃) for 25 min, the mixtures were put into ascorbic acid (VC) and heated at 95 ℃ for 1 h. Finally, the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH as ternary photocatalyst was cleaned with deionized water to remove useless residual and PEG before freeze-drying, which were denoted respectively as Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH(2%), Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH(4%), Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH(6%), Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH(8%), Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH(10%). The differences in ratio illustrated the mass of rGH in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, which the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (6%) were selected for further experiment.
The characterization methods, photoelectrochemical characterization and photocatalysis experiments were shown in Supporting Information [21].

The crystal structure analysis of the rGH, Ag3PO4, N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst were illustrated through XRD patterns (Fig. 1 ). A wide peak of rGH (2θ = 24.5°) disappeared in the spectrum of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, and this indicated that disordered graphene nano-sheets layers in rGH would be filled uniformly by N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 forming a network space. In addition, the broad diffraction peak (d002) at 2θ = 27.12° of N, P-CQDs could be ignored in the ordered phases of binary catalysts (Fig. S2a). All diffraction peaks of N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH could be directly accorded with the cubic phase of Ag3PO4 (JCPDS card no. 06-0505). It was a tiny peak (2θ = 38.1°) showed in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH pattern which was a relation to the (1 1 1) panel from Ag (JCPDS card no. 04-0783). The changes indicated that Ag+ was slightly reduced to Ag by reducer VC during the step of rGH synthesis. The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH exposed more (1 1 0) facets than N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4. The intensity of (2 1 0) phases in N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 was the weakest because of inlaid N, P-CQDs. The incremental high-energy (1 1 0)/(2 1 0) surfaces could strengthen the photocatalytic activity [14].

To investigate the microstructures and morphology details of as-prepared samples, the products were characterized by SEM and HRTEM. The morphology of pure Ag3PO4 samples with an average size of 0.28 μm was measured. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1a, a few spherical particles were dispersed on cubic polyhedron. rGH were porousness and possessed large accessible surface area (Fig. 2b and Fig. S1b). Compared with pure Ag3PO4, the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 had blunt edges (Fig. 2c). HRTEM image (Fig. 2e) detailly displayed that the surface of Ag3PO4 cube was inlayed equably by N, P-CQDs. The SEM and TEM image of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (Fig. 2d and Fig. S1c) showed that the rGH network acted as a supporter to distribute N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 catalyst. The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH remained steady particles and expanded photocatalytic reaction area due to the 3D network space, which could be kept the structure after photocatalyst recyclability test (Fig. S1d). The detailed components of N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was analyzed by HRTEM (Fig. 2f and Fig. S1e). The resolved lattice fringes of Ag3PO4 and N, P-CQDs was 0.268 nm and 0.213 nm, corresponding to (2 1 0) planes of as-prepared silver phosphate and the (1 0 0) diffraction facet of graphite carbon, respectively [22,23]. The clearly lattice spacing in binary composite catalyst indicated that the N, P-CQDs was loaded on Ag3PO4. Additionally, the N, P-CQDs connected with rGH and Ag3PO4 in the ternary composite catalyst, while the rGH was amorphism causing the unclearly interplanar spacing. The interplanar distance d consisted with XRD. In addition, the size of N, P-CQDs with an average diameter of about 4.5 nm was counted by TEM spectrum (Fig. S1f), which the specific interlayer spacing distance was 0.213 nm (Fig. S2b).

The surface chemical states of elements amongst Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH were characterized by XPS. The existence of P, C, Ag, N, O elements in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was shown in the survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a). The P2p peak position at 132.7 eV was phosphorus on the surface of Ag3PO4 or N, P-CQDs [25,26]. The two strong peaks (374.45 and 368.55 eV) of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 in Ag 3d XPS spectra testified that Ag + was major states in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (Fig. 4b). There were two lower banding energy peaks at 374.6 and 368.45 eV should be observed due to the responding of Ag0, because the zero-valent silver was slightly translated by the reduction of Ag+ . This result was similar to the XRD analysis. The C1s spectra could be divided into five peaks, which the banding energy was shown as Fig. 4c. The existence of epoxy/hydroxyls and oxygen-containing functional in rGH represented similarly in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH. The particular amounts of C-O (286.9 eV) and O = C-OH (287.7 eV) are benefit in the photocatalytic activity [8]. Notably, the interactions between N, P-CQDs and rGH mainly caused the formation of C-N peaks (285.4 eV) accelerating the electron transfer [27], which this result of ternary photocatalyst structure was similarly conformed to HRTEM. The C-N chemical bonds could simultaneously improve the electronic properties and reactivity of the N, P-CQDs (Fig. S4a and b) [28]. The banding energies of the O elements in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH revealed the existence of C-O-C/C-OH (534.2 eV), P-O-P/O-CO (533.6 eV), OC-OH (532.9 eV) and CO (531.7 eV) in Fig. 4c [29], in which the presence of P-O-P/O-CO and CO were on the surface of N, P-CQDs (Fig. S4c) [30]. The further discussion from spectrum of N1s and P2p of N, P-CQDs illustrated that the N and P doped on the CQDs successfully (Fig. S4d and e). Three main peaks at 402.7, 401.5 and 400 eV were endued in the form of H-N (sp3), C-N-C and C3-N on the surface of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (Fig. 4e). In the high-resolution spectrum of P2p (Fig. 4f), the only weak peaks at 132.7 eV was endued in the form of P-O on the surface of ternary composite catalyst, which indicated the covalently bonded PO4 3− group [30]. These hydrophilic groups on the surface of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH were came from N, P-CQDs. Combining with the results of FTIR (Fig. S5), the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH that existed the hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl, amidogen, carbonyl and phosphate) excited more photocarrier generation and accelerated charge separation [31]. The inside interaction between binary catalyst and conductive rGH provided further electron transfer reaction and more active absorption sites throughout the network space to strengthen photo-stability and photocatalytic activity.

The optical adsorption and bandgap properties were described by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 5 a). The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH displayed the highest absorbance in the visible-light region (>420 nm) after N, P-CQDs and rGH cooperated. To comparison with single Ag3PO4, the absorption edge of binary catalysts and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH showed red shifting with different degree. The absorption region was broadened in ternary photocatalyst via eliminating the shadowing effect of the rGH, when the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 dispersed uniformly. According to the results of DRS, the band gap energy values was calculated from the axis intercept which the linear part transformed by Kulblka-Munk function (Fig. 5b). The values of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (2.42 eV) and N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 (2.53 eV) was narrowed comparing to the Ag3PO4 (3.64 eV), indicating the electron were excited more easily. The results described that the N, P-CQDs and rGH interacted with Ag3PO4 would take full advantage of visible light sources. To evaluate the conduction band (CB) edge position and the valence band (VB) edge position in the ternary catalyst, the Mott-Schottky (MS) plots and VB XPS spectra were measured in Fig. S6a and b. The CB potentials of N, P-CQDs and Ag3PO4 was −0.76 eV and 0.25 eV respectively. The VB edge position of N, P-CQDs and Ag3PO4 were estimated to be 0.61 eV and 2.67 eV respectively.

PL spectra of three photocatalysts were displayed in Fig. 5c (excitation wavelength at 450 nm). The N, P-CQDs as an exciter on the surface of Ag3PO4 increased the light quantum and electron transfer efficiency to result in the obvious quenching of PL intensity, ranking as Ag3PO4 > N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 > Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH. The peak intensity of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was lowest, because the intermolecular bond between the rGH and the exterior N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 made electrons transfer further increase. Moreover, the recombination of e−­h+ pairs was weakened due to the decreased intensity in PL spectra [32]. The gradual enhancement photocurrent density evidenced more photogenerated charge carriers through the interface of the network space (Fig. S6c). The electrochemical impedance (EIS) was carried out under visible light to further effectively analyze the charge process of photo-generated electron/hole pairs (Fig. S6d). The Nyquist plots of Ag3PO4, N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH could indicate the charge transfer resistance in which the smallest semicircular diameter suggested the fastest separation and transfer of photogeneration carriers over the ternary photocatalyst [33]. Furthermore, the N, P-CQDs who possessed the effective tunable photoluminescence (DCPL and UCPL) and the unique photoinduced electrons transfer effect played a role in improving the photocatalysis activity [34,35]. The hydrophilic groups which researched in XPS of N, P-CQDs could cause the photoluminescence. The light of higher (>500 nm) or lower (<4 2 0) wavelength turn to the region of wavelength absorbed by Ag3PO4 (420–530 nm) [36,37] (Fig. 5d and Fig. S7). In conclusion, this reasonable design among the three materials made great contribution to enhancing the photocatalytic performance and anti-photocorrosion.

To confirm the absorption ability of the photocatalysts, an adsorption-desorption equilibrium experiment was carried out (Fig. S8). Owing to the successful introduction of rGH, the photocatalysts exhibited the highest TC adsorption capacity (16.55 mg/g). There was almost one time as many adsorption rates in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH as N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4, which the absorption area was enlarged by the composite N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 particles on the network structure of rGH. The results of the absorption test matched BET detection. In the dark reaction process, the TC adsorption by Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH generally saturated in 45 min. The different TC removal efficiency of as-prepared photocatalysts was available (Fig. 6 ). The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH showed the highest TC removal efficiency of ca.94.52% in 60 min, while the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 exhibited lower efficiency (ca.80.03%). The pure Ag3PO4 showed the lowest photocatalytic performance for degradation TC of only ca.75.00%. By comparison, the photocatalysis activity of N, P-CQDs and rGH appeared negligible. When the light was on, the degradation effect in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was stronger than the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 after 20 min. Admittedly, the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH maintained sustained degradation. Finally, the contaminant could be mineralized better, rather than enriching in the photocatalysts, where the degree of mineralization was concluded the total organic carbon (TOC) removal (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters in the photodegradation reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalyst dosage, content of rGH, initial TC concentrations and light irradiation effects were conducted for photocatalyst of optimum design. The effect of dosage was investigated at ranging from 0.05 to 1.00 (Fig. 7 a). The TC removal efficiency increased sharply when the catalysis dosages increased from 0.05 to 0.2 g/L, but the efficiency cut down while the further increase of catalysis amount. Hence, the superfluous catalyst dosage led to the physical shadowing effect and the moderate dosage 0.2 g/L was suggested. The TC degradation rate was 94.52%, 92.81%, 80.72%, 61.80%, 51.95%, where the initial concentrations of 10 to 80 mg/L respectively (Fig. 7b), which showed an efficient photocatalysis even in high concentration within 60 min. The colored solution might weaken photo-permeability resulting in the retardation of surface photon entering. The adsorption and catalytic sites were occupied by intermediates of decomposing TC, while the rest of the TC degradation was restricted [38]. The excellent specific surface and adsorption property in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH decreased this restriction. The degradation suggested that the composite with the rGH content of 6% exhibited a best catalytic activity (Fig. 7c). The N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 can hardly disperse when the layer of interconnected 3D network structure unformed or accumulated (Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (2%) or Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (10%)). The cumulated layer of graphene prevented the photogenerated charge transference for removal pollutants [24,39]. An experiment was settled with or without the optical cut-off filter (λ < 420 nm) in Fig. 7d. The highest photodegradation TC efficiency with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH of 95.1% under full spectrum condition (λ > 365). Besides, the composites of rGH/Ag3PO4 without N, P-CQDs was visibly obtained the difference between the TC removal degradation (with light filter: 65%; without light filter: 53.7%). The N, P-CQDs could broaden the light response range of Ag3PO4 to develop the photocatalysis activity. Combining with the results of XPS and FTIR, the graphene hydrogel in the ternary composites would accelerate the electron transfer for improving photocatalysis activity but not convert the wavelength.

The effect of various water quality parameters was conducted for analyzing the photocatalysis degradation system in real wastewater. The degradation ratio was 90.10%, 94.23%, 79.23%, 76.32% and 83.73% in tap water, de-ionized water, medical water, municipal wastewater and river water respectively (Fig. 8 a). Certainly, the existing of competitive parameters (reducing substances and organics) from the various actual water sources were investigated (Table S1). The slight reduction of the photodegradation efficiencies might be caused by common anions. The photoexcitation process can be influenced by the initial pH of reaction solution (Fig. 8b). The optimal pH value was at ca. 6.78 and the suppression of degradation was particularly in acid condition. To further authenticate the effect of the pH (3–11) to Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, the corresponding Zeta potentials before or after photodegradation have been listed (Fig. 8c). As a negative surface charge photocatalysts, Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH strengthened the electrostatic attraction between the TC molecules with opposite positively charged to enhanced the adsorption ability [40]. Nevertheless, the more electrostatic attraction might block the generation of optical carriers on the surface. It is wildly accepted that sulfate, chlorine, bicarbonate ions and hydrogen phosphate ions are familiar anions in real wastewater. Herein, the effect of simulant supporting electrolyte (Na2SO4, NaCl, Na2CO3 and Na2HPO4) was investigated on photocatalysis over Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (Fig. 8d). The obvious negative effect of TC removal was Na2CO3 electrolyte, because the carbonate ions were the radical scavengers [41,42]. The sulfate, chlorine and hydrogen phosphate ions are competitive with TC molecules in the absorption sites on the network space. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH in various supporting electrolytes putted in the following order: NaCl > Na2SO4 > Na2HPO4 > Na2CO3 (Fig. S10). It suggested that the more negative potential would make the competitive photocatalysis activity. The abundant active absorption sites helped the TC to eliminate the competitive ion and maintained the photodegradation efficiency above ca.75% in a relevant real water matrix. Therefore, optimized photocatalysts is potentiality in the treatment of actual wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of photocatalysts: cycling test and effect of water source test
ce:section.ce:para: The circulate degradation test with binary catalysts and ternary photocatalyst was performed for five consecutive TC degradation cycles (Fig. 9 a and b). The Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH were more stable than the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 remaining about 90.83% (only about 0.47% loss) and 37.33% (about 43.7% lost) respectively after the fifth cycle. In further studies, the degradation rate of pure Ag3PO4 was from 75% of the first cycle to 35.4% of the fifth cycle, which was losing 39.6% (Fig. S11). The N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 particles could be steadied by rGH as a network for better photostability. In Fig. 9c and Fig. S12, after the sixth circulation, the photodegradation efficiency over Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was almost retained in different wastewater. By contrast, the degradation efficiency of TC over N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and pure Ag3PO4 was substantially smaller than Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH in various water sources. This decrease was specially performed in medical water and municipal wastewater, furthermore the less TC removal ability by used N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 and Ag3PO4 was 7.68% and 5.23% respectively. Therefore, advanced photostability of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH potentially possessed a long-term exercisable wastewater purification for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergy of adsorption and photocatalytic stability
ce:section.ce:para: The stability performance of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH and N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 with absorption, photolysis and synergetic degradation of photocatalysis and adsorption for 60 min at 6 times cyclic experiment (Fig. 9d and Fig. S13). For the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, the adsorption ratio of TC was 37% for the first time, while the ratio dropped considerably to 8.2% till the fourth time. The pollutants occupied the adsorption sites in the photocatalysts made restrictions on the further TC removed. Therefore, the only adsorption test tended to become none after four cycles, in which the mineralization of organic pollution was basically depended on photocatalysis activity. The photolysis of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was a smooth decline in the percentage of photocatalytic degradation form 81.5% in the first time to 74.6% in the final time. In contrast, the binary catalysts were lost significantly 36.6% at the sixth cycle owing to photocorrosion. Where the photocatalysis experiment was operated with synergetic adsorption and photocatalysis, the TC removal efficiency in each cycling experiment was the greatest (rates up to 91.5% in Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH, 82.4% in N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4). The synergetic efficiency was more obvious in ternary photocatalyst because of the gel with absorptivity. The mineralization rate was less than 10% at first 60 min (Fig. S9). Subsequently, the rate after 60 min decreased to 1.95% compared to the data at 30 min (2.67%), and the TOC in the dark reaction system had an upward trend resulting from intermediate products desorption. The catalysts adsorption behavior was saturated after 6 times recycle, but the synergy reaction could deplete the extra pollutions by photocatalytic degradation and keep circulation. The above results indicated that the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH materials shown a favorable stability in the absorption and photocatalytic synergetic reaction to achieve the continuous organic pollutants treatment in the future reality.

In order to learn the changes of internal structures in photocatalyst after photocatalysis reaction, the XRD and FTIR of binary photocatalyst and Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH after 6th reaction was characterized (Fig. 9 e and f). Firstly, the N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 did not discover the presence of pure Ag until the photocatalysis reaction happened, which the intensity value of N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 is much higher than Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH. The diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was maintained before or after photodegradation. The main absorption peaks of fresh N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4 at 1640 cm−1, 1383 cm−1, 1066 cm−1 and 550 cm−1 was transfer completely after photocatalysis. The internal crystalloid and chemical characteristic structure of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH remain constantly. As an electron reservoir, the N, P-CQDs could trap photoelectron to reduce electron-hole pairs’ recombination. As an electric conductor, the rGH could guide photoelectron to flow throughout the network for photostability. In conclusion, the photogenerated carriers can be separated efficiently with Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH for anti-photocorrosion.

The possible degradation pathway of TC was analyzed using LC-MS techniques (Fig. 10 ). The mass peaks at m/z 445.1 was TC, while its intensity massively reduced after 60 min (Fig. S14). This change testified that the TC was adequately degraded to some mineralization products and the corresponding intermediates were showed in Fig. S15. According to the generated intermediate and reported studies, it could propose three pathways for TC photodegradation: Pathway Ⅰ, the production 1 (m/z = 475.3) was rearranged by the hydroxyl radical and N-methyl from TC (m/z = 445.1) to become N-aldehyde group at C11a–C12 double-bond, because of the presence of oxidant (h+). The production 2 (m/z = 437.1) was generated through –OH which was displaced C-N bonds [43,44]; Pathway Ⅱ displayed a highly efficient holonomic mineralization process of TC. Firstly, the TC molecule was dehydrated and simultaneously peeled off hydroxyl oxidizing to production 3 (m/z = 427.1). The production 4 was shucked off –NH2 from production 3 and this process might be a benefit for further hydrolyzation to become production 5 [45,46]. The aromatic ring from production 6 (m/z = 321) and production (m/z = 276) could be easily attacked by superoxide radical to produce low-molecular-weight organics [47]. Finally, the 3-hexanone and alkanolamine was degraded by further oxidation to achieve the TC mineralization [48]. Pathway III was multi-step fragmentation of TC by reactive species. After the deamidation reaction of TC into production 10, the production 11 (m/z = 318.2) was generated via the dehydroxylation, opening of benzene rings, deethylation and addition reaction. The production 11 translated to production 12 (m/z = 274.2) by deacetylation and oxidation reaction [49].

The principal active species generated from Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalysis system were researched through typical trapping experiments (Fig. 11 a). It has been proved that the 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) and Isopropanol (IPA) can serve as the scavenger to catch the superoxide radical (
O2 −), hole (h+) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) respectively in a batch model [42,50]. It cannot be denied that the uppermost active group was holes when the photodegradation efficiency of TC substantially cut down from 90.30% to 35.94% after the addition of 1 mM EDTA-2Na. A similar result was obtained in the presence of BQ. However only 8.56% inhibition was observed when the scavenger was IPA. These results suggested that the TC was mainly oxidized by the
O2 − and h+ instead of
OH. To further research the superoxide radical as an important role in the TC oxidation, a N2 purging experiment was carried out. The degradation efficiency of TC was prevented under N2 atmosphere, which the removal efficiency decreased by 35% than air-equilibrated conditions. The result showed that the dissolved oxygen as an electron scavenger played a crucial role in production pathway of
O2 − radical species. With the increasing intensity of DMPO-
O2 − adducts signal from dark to visible light irritation, the principal active species
O2 − was worked in this system (Fig. 11b) [51]. The signal of TEMPO-h+ for Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH was more weaken when the light was on, then the TEMPO-h+ was no signal at the 10 min (Fig. 11c) and the signal of DMPO-
OH was the weakest within 10 min (Fig. 11d). Normally, the H2O or OH− can be oxidized by photo proton to produce the
OH. However, in this network reaction space, the organic pollutant was directly oxidized by h+, which the generation of hydroxyl radical was reduced by easy oxidation process [52]. The h+ and
O2 − principal active species was similarly expounded from the ESR spin-trap techniques under dark and visible light irradiation of 5 min and 10 min.

Based on the above results, the mechanism in the Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalysis system with 3D network space structure was proposed (Scheme 1 and Fig. S16). The excellent synergetic photocatalysis activity and stability effect was attributed to the high surface area of network structure and special vector electron transfer. The compatible wavelength light was converted by N, P-CQDs for exciting more photon-generated carrier in Ag3PO4. The stable reaction network space between gel and binary catalysts stored the TC after saturated absorption and transferred the photo-generated carriers to accelerate the mass transport. The h+ remained on the VB of Ag3PO4 reacted with organic pollutants. Specially, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 could transfer into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of N, P-CQDs as electron reservoirs for charge-carriers separation in the Ag3PO4 [53]. In band alignment of Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH (Fig. S16), the more negative CB of N, P-CQDs (-0.76 eV) could generated more
O2 − [54]. Afterward, the photoelectron would be reserved quickly in network space and sequentially transferred to the surface of photocatalysis system for the redox reaction [55]. A large amount of the dissolved O2 could be captured to form an oxidizing species
O2 − on the surface or in the space for TC photo-degradation system. The TC removal process on exterior of ternary photocatalyst was supposed to the multi-step electrons transfer, where the accumulated electron flowed as Ag3PO4 → N, P-CQDs → rGH [36].

Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst with network space structure possessed outstanding synergy effect, anti-photocorrosion and recyclability. The N, P-CQDs of large-scale synthesis with self-adjustment photoluminescence properties can activated the high-energy facets on Ag3PO4 to produce more photocarriers. The photogenerated electrons could transfer to carbon nanostructures (N, P-CQDs) which simultaneously trapped electrons and accelerated photocarriers separation. On the other hand, the abundant hydrophilic functional groups in rGH helped the strong interfacial contact with binary catalysts, which made electron flow to rGH constantly. The inlayed “spacer particles” (N, P-CQDs/Ag3PO4) between the graphene sheets could control the π-π stacking interaction and Vander Waals force to construct a special network space structure. In reaction network space for photodegradation, the TC were directly oxidized by the holes and the current photo electron caused superoxide radical chain reaction. The TC removal performance over Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst was optimized in a relevant real water matrix, which exhibited effective mineralization and stability. In conclusion, this Ag3PO4/NP-CQDs/rGH photocatalyst was rationally designed for environment remediation, which not only indicated the photocatalytic activity/stability synergy for highly organic pollutants degradation efficiency but also supplied comprehensive insights for the photo functional nanodevices in practical application.
